sentences.text: Right.
Right.
Burned alive.
There were many, of course, who had died of starvation in the camp, who had been just allowed to lie around.
They had no time to dispose of them.
But the ones who had been burned, you can see that they died in agony.
As you'll see in the pictures that we're going to show, their arms were up flung.
They were screaming and dying.
We didn't have exact numbers, but somebody figured that of the 8,000-- and that was a small camp, designated Jews, political prisoners, by the way-- of the 8,000, all but 3,000 had been killed.
Shot and burned to death.
And this, I emphasize, was done in a totally senseless manner.
It couldn't accomplish anything, even in their own perverted ends.
It was just killing to kill.
They didn't want these people to be liberated.
They didn't want the victory to be the-- to be the-- in the inmates.
And they wanted a final victory of a perverted, savage end, so they just killed most of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our division-- the whole division caught up.
Our division headquarters people in the military government rallied around.
They opened a hospital for them, and took over buildings, and rallied medical support for those who were still alive.
The big concern, of course, was about typhus, which was untreatable in those days.
And we only stayed two days because we were combat elements.
We went right on.
But the-- they got the mayor and a bunch of German civilians out from town, marched them out, made them look at the whole thing, and made them dig graves, mass graves for the dead.
And they had a burial service.

Of course, nobody knew who these people were, or had the vaguest notion of what their religion was, so they had a rabbi, a-- a Protestant chaplain, and a priest each read their respective services, and we buried the dead.

And of course, the usual-- we have pictures of the German civilians carrying the-- the dead, and the thing to notice is that the German civilians were fat, muscular, well fed, with no signs of any malnutrition.
The standard excuse for the camps, of course, by those who would deny them is that everybody was on short rations.
Not the German civilians.
They were the best fed people in Europe, because they had looted all of Europe.
And the-- the contrast in the pictures is all too plain.
They were pretty horrified.
They knew it was bad, but I don't think they were prepared for just how bad it was.
Some of them got sick.
Some of them got hysterical.
And they kept-- the standard saying, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- we never suspected, we never suspected.
Over and over again they kept saying it.
We didn't suspect it.
We didn't believe it.
We didn't know what was going on.
Oh, they were gone.
They'd run away.
When the American machine guns got anywhere near, they took to their heels, and left their victims behind.
They were not only murderers, but cowards.
Not of that group.
We got the story from the other prisoners of what had happened.
Oh yeah, sure.
Totally mixed.
I didn't see any in that particular camp.
Well, they-- they were the same as the people I saw in Dachau, they were unlike anything I've seen in 50 years as a physician.
I have seen people die of about every disease you can think of.
And I've seen every kind of starvation and malnutrition, as a result of a disease primarily.
But I've never seen anything like these people.
Again, they were so thin that the faces looked like bird's faces, the bones projected.
The arms were just bones, barely covered.
The knees, the joints, were protuberant.
The leg would be tiny and thin, and then there'd be a great big ball socket of the joint, and then tiny, thin, little rail of bone going up above it.
I couldn't understand quite how they even stood, the poor people, or how they could move, but they could do it.
I didn't have time for a lot of examination at Landsberg, the way I did at Dachau, because we-- we were in the combat elements, and on we went.
But our division rear people did a pretty good, credible job of cleaning it up, and getting the dead buried, and the living into hospitals, and getting food to everybody.
The young Jewish boy that I met first is the only one I remember vividly out of a sea of faces.
The one who told me his mother and father both had been burned, burned, burned.
And he's the one that sticks in my mind, standing there clutching that, what was left of that loaf of bread, chewing on it, and crying as hard as he could cry.
Terrible sight.
No, no.
This was a simple work and death camp.
They were put there to be starved and worked to death, is what they really were.
And the killing was incidental.
In other words, they saw the Allies approaching, and the orders were to kill them all.
That's kind of camp it was.
Pardon?
No.
Nothing like that.
Again, we-- we-- we were not-- were there briefly, as I say, I had to go back and arrange for the attack the next morning, day after that.
Not for the prisoners.
I guess they had a little dispensary or something, but it was so crude you wouldn't want to use it.
Our people just took over a German building in the town and turned it into a hospital.
Never did find out who was responsible for all this.
I have a feeling the military government people may have discovered it, or the people who were doing the de-nazification studied later in Germany.
The guards took their heels so fast, there was no question of catching this particular group.
They were gone.
Well, after that, we attacked south.
And our division seized the first bridge over the Danube intact.
You know, the Danube runs east and west there.
And as you came south, suddenly there were the Bavarian Alps.
Just came around the corner one day and there the sky was full of mountains.
And we thought, oh, god, we have to fight our way through those.
Because the thought was the Germans were dug into the Bavarian Alps, which they weren't.
So they-- at the division commanders'-- battalion commanders' meeting that morning, someone tried some clumsy humor.
The commanding general said, gentleman, there's a river 40 miles south of here, and I hear that it's blue.
Go check it out.
He meant the beautiful blue Danube, of course.
And that backfired on him.
So we took off and streamed toward the Danube.
Three columns, CCB on the left, CR on the right, CC in the middle.
And we all-- it had a bridge that we were going to try to get intact before the Germans could blow it up.

And an odd thing happened as we went streaming south, we suddenly heard-- the radio started to chatter from CCR, Combat Command Reserve-- We got a bridge, we got a bridge, we got a bridge over the Danube.
Like, holy cow.
You know, intact, seizing it.
It was like the bridge they seized at the Rhine a couple months before.
And in fact, they had.
The Task Force [?
Ippolito, ?] under a little Italian New York kid named Ippolito, went into the town with guns blazing and shot up everything, and the Germans dived in their hole.

They ran across the bridge, they got the demolitions, which were big airplane bombs wired to go up, and they cut the wires before they could explode them, and threw them in the Danube, and we had a bridge over the Danube.
Well, the odd thing was it got in the army histories.
Then, the radio said, hey, the bridge is blown.
We looked, we are just a couple-- when a bridge goes up, you see it from a long way off.
We said, it didn't blow.

And all the confusion went back, the bridge over the Danube, went all the way to army headquarters, Seventh Army, everyone was-- the bridge-- they had a bridge, but it got blown, and it's all in the history books.
And then it turned out, no it wasn't.
Ippolito ?] remembered what he heard that morning at the briefing.
He said we've got a ridge over the river, and it's blue.
And this was turned into blown.
So anyway, we pulled up in the-- for the night at the picture I've shown you, at the Castle Hochstadt, which is a 1,000-year-old castle that Napoleon had used for headquarters.
And I went riding out with the tanks while we shot up some Germans who were trying to lay mine fields on the other side against our crossing.
We crossed over, and we did some heavy fighting for two days south of the Danube, and then broke out toward the Alps.
And suddenly, we came over a hill, and I could see German prisoners as far as you could see them.
Thousands and thousands and thousands, the whole German army marching in demoralized-- beaten, weaponless, hands up, marching by-- I couldn't believe how many Germans I saw marching to the rear.
And we pushed south toward the Danube-- beyond the Danube to the Alps.
And our last goal was the next valley over from Oberammergau, the famous place where the Passion Play is put on every year.
And there we were in the middle of the towering snow peaks.
And that was our last position.
We were ordered to hold in place.

One of our combat commands of our division went racing on, and they liberated a fort where they were holding many distinguished prisoners, the premier of France and many other people, whom they had not quite killed yet.
And they got into the castle, and the SS troops who were behind them attacked them.
And the odd thing was a German officer fought the SS troops to help them try and save the prisoners.
There were some decent Germans.
And he was killed in the fighting, but they-- then our column came up, drove the SS off, and they saved the-- the premier of France, Daladier, and a bunch of other distinguished people.
Another team, at that point in the war, said they found some crazy Kraut scientist hiding in a cave talking about a wind tunnel or something.
It was one of Wernher von Braun's rocket teams.
They were running away from the Russians.
And we picked them up.
Top priority.
We-- our-- the Allies wanted them.
So we picked up Wernher von Braun.
And one of-- part of our division captured von Rundstedt, the famous German general who commanded the Battle of the Bulge.
Marched him in.
So we were picking up big game at the end of the war.
I didn't see Rundstedt.
He was too far.
I wanted to go give him a hard time, but I didn't get to.
They had marched him back when I got over there.
So the war ended there, and we sat in place.
May-- Well, we stopped advancing about May 4.
There was no more resistance.
We were told to hold in place.
The Germans in south-- southeast army front surrendered a day or two ahead of the regular surrender.
And we worked with some veterans of the Yugoslav regular army, who had been prisoners of war, slave laborers, and gave them guns.
They-- they-- the only prisoners we ever liberated.
They went out in the mountains hunting up the SS troops, and they loved it.
They'd flush them out and bring them in terrified with their hands over their heads.
They were tough people, Yugoslavs.
So we-- then, on May 7, the big surrender was signed.
But before that, we pulled back.
We met the Fifth Army coming up from Italy.
The war was over, as far as we were concerned.
They pulled us back north of the Danube.
And we're sitting in a town called [?
Wasseralfingen ?] in northern Bavaria, and the news came over the radio that the surrender at Reims.
Donitz, the German admiral had surrendered.
No underground, no werewolves, no resistance, it's all over.
And I walked into the command post where the colonel and three battalion commanders were planning roadblocks and operations, and I said, gentlemen, by the way, the war just ended.
And they looked at me and said, oh, yeah, thanks, doc, OK, and went right on planning.
They couldn't stop what they were doing.
[LAUGHS] Finally, that night we celebrated.
But we were up at a city called Dillingen on the Danube where we had gotten the bridge.
We-- they moved-- shuffled us around, a couple days after the surrender.
And a young Captain Levy, Matt [?
Levy ?] from New York, [INAUDIBLE], stopped in to have lunch at our officer's mess.
When you travel you stopped in at other officer's mess for lunch, or something.
And he told me he was working at Dachau, in the typhus center.
He described the conditions there, and the food problems they were having, and things like that.
So I-- I got like I ought to help out.
I had no more wounded to take care of.
I had nothing to do in my official capacity.
We had plenty of medical personnel for the kids that got sick or something.
So with the consent of my commanding officer, I took my ambulance and drove through Augsburg and down to Dachau, to the 66th Field Hospital, which was the typhus center.
Introduced myself to the colonel, commanding officer, and he said, fine, major, always glad to have extra hands.
Welcome aboard.
So I got sprayed with DDT to kill lice, of course.
And went to work in the typhus hospital, stayed in the barracks there.
I spent two weeks in Dachau, in the typhus center.
You want me to tell about that?
Yes, I do.
Well, typhus is the disease that's transmitted by the bite of a louse.
And it's Rickettsial disease, a germ that's the littlest thing you can see with an ordinary microscope.
Bigger than a virus, smaller than bacteria.
I causes Rocky Mountain spotted fever.
It causes many different kinds of disease.
In those days there was no treatment.
Nowadays, you can take -mycin, any of the -mycins, and you're well in 48 hours.
In those days, it was usually half the time fatal.
Of course, the prisoners were lousy.
The filthy conditions in these camps, they had-- they had lice.
And typhus broke out everywhere.

So the-- the field hospital was set up in a great big barracks, one of those big things with the sign that said, 'Arbeit macht frei'-- 'work will make you free.' The nastiest joke ever pulled on a bunch of unfortunate people.
And their beds-- I guess there were 40 in a row, like a big gymnasium, they were on cots lying there.
And they set up-- this was a real field hospital, rear installation.
They had nurses, medical personnel, proper intravenous equipment, things like that.
And excuse me, I'm getting caught in this wire here.
There.
So that-- these people, what-- they all looked like the people, the living, I had seen at Landsberg.
Again, they were skeletons.
Just skeletons.
And speaking as a medical man, I was curious, I asked them-- I'd take-- I'd take-- put my fingers around the arm.
You could put your fingers around a man's upper arm just like that.
And I'd say, [SPEAKING GERMAN], move your arm.
And they could move it, but I couldn't feel any muscle.
I didn't know what they moved it with.
All I felt was bone there.
Starved beyond belief.
They-- many of them had tuberculosis, of course.
They had starvation.
They had a condition called beriberi heart, which is what you get from a lack of thyamine, vitamin B1.
The heart gets huge and then goes into failure.
So then, on the little X-ray machine, we had these huge hearts that half filled the chest.
So they were starved, they had beriberi, they had tuberculosis, and then they got typhus.
So they died like flies.
We could give them intravenous fluids.
We could give them drugs like digitalis to strengthen the heart.
We could give them adrenaline when the blood pressure fell too low.
But that was about all we could do.
What we could do is very limited.
And feed them.
Feeding them was the big thing.
That made the enormous difference.
As soon as they could get some food in, some of them got better.
And people who were in Dachau were every race you can think of.
I didn't see any black people, but I saw everybody else you could possibly think of.
Every country in Europe, every country in the Western world.
There were some Americans in Dachau, but I didn't see any of them, in other parts of the camp.
And they told me story after story of the kind I'm sure you've heard over and over again, of being picked up for their race, half of them, or religion, in some cases, or political beliefs.
One of the people I visited with most was a Dutch physician who been a member of the Dutch Communist Party.
Of course, he gets snapped up early on.
And he was staggering and almost falling down.
But he insisted on sitting up with us reading X-rays and filling his role as a physician once again.
It was very wonderful to see.
I hope he-- I hope he lived.
But as we walked down the aisle-- the-- along the aisle of beds, our chief helper was a young Yugoslav surgeon, who was a prisoner himself.
But he hadn't been in long enough to be starved very much.
So he had an American army uniform.
We called him 'Titto Minor,' which made him very proud.
And he was a graduate of the University of Belgrade.
And he knew about typhus, because he-- he was part of the partisan army, the guerrillas.
Of course, they were lousy, and they-- he knew typhus when he saw it.
The Americans didn't.
They've never seen a case.
They-- so he would go along-- with typhus, you get spots.
The blood vessels rupture and they leave spots on the skin.
Fleck typhus it's called, spotted typhus.
And he could call the shots better than any of us.
He'd say, [SPEAKING GERMAN], more spots, the tongue is dry, he'll be dead tonight.
And they were.
The way typhus works is that the germ attacks the tiny blood vessels called capillaries, the littlest ones that you rupture when you have a nose bleed.
And any part of your body.
If it attacks in the gastrointestinal tract, you vomit blood, and you pass blood in the stool, and you die.
If it's in the lungs, you cough blood and hemorrhage in your lung and die.
And we saw both types.
People's lungs would just fill up with blood, and there wasn't anything we could do about it except keep them comfortable.
The gastrointestinal type was particularly horrible to take care of as you can imagine.
Blood coming out both ends.
And sometimes it attacks the central nervous system.
I wrote about that in my book.
The spine-- the brain cord-- the spinal cord and brain.
And this one pathetic case I'll remember to my dying day, this man had typhus of the spinal cord and brain, and he was convulsing, like a man with lockjaw.
And he drew himself up in a bow, so that just his heels and the back of his head touched the bed.
The rest of his body was just taut, like that, screaming at the top of his lungs.
Then he fell dead, wham, just while we looked at him.
The death rate was so high that we always knew, as we went along each row of beds, which was, say, 40-some, as I recall, some would be dead that we'd passed that had been alive when we went by.
It was incredibly high.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The stories, of course, were endless.
I was picked up because I was a Jew.
It was very common.
And I've been worked here and worked there.
And the stories-- the second-hand stories I heard of the beatings and shootings and the casual murders were blood curdling, and I guess you've heard plenty of stories like that.
Well, one of-- the one man told me about being in one of those railway boxcars, when they locked-- you know, they slammed people in a boxcar so tightly they couldn't breathe.
No sanitation, no water or anything, and he was one of, what, half a dozen people that lived when they opened the doors and the dead bodies fell out.
And that was-- one man told-- many of them had been in the transport trains, and they all had pretty much the same story.
They were the 10% that survived that little process.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they would talk about certain guards.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], they'd say.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], frightful, frightful, who would be just sadists, who were allowed to take out their sadism on prisoners.

Some-- they made a big distinction between a-- a guard that was not too bad and a guard that was horrible, the guards that just carried out orders and the ones that went way beyond, they just killed people.
And I heard many, many, they'd say-- those who had been in a camp together someplace.
You know, they moved them around the different camps for various reasons.
I never understood why.
They'd say, oh, so at Auschwitz or something, oh, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
A particular guard.
Many of the guards that were the worst, according to the prisoners, were Ukrainians, by the way, which is a fact not sufficiently appreciated today.
The-- you'd mention Ukrainer, and they all start swearing and cursing.
They had all had terrible treatment from Ukrainian guards, who worked for the Germans.
Apparently, anti-Semitism is a way of life in the Ukraine for a lot of people.
[DRINKS] And they were unanimous in saying-- if possible-- is it a possibly worse than a German-- they'd say, yeah, Ukrainer is worse.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And of course, starvation continued to be a problem on the limited rations we could get for them, till we finally got enough food, thank god, and some of them started to get better.
Army rations.
They-- there was a lot of administrative confusion at first.
The first thing they did was to just get C-rations, K, stuff we ate, which was pretty awful.
The combat troops.
C-rations, K-rations, 10-in-1, put them in a big stew pot, mix it up, and feed them out of it.
Then, the regulations, they found regulations, well, you can't use army rations, and yadda, yadda, yadda, and they were trying to get the German civilians to bring in food.
I could've told them that was a waste of time.
But they finally had a big captured enemy warehouse in Munich.
They were able to get food from that.
So before I left, the food situation had straightened itself out pretty well.
And they were-- I remember one prisoner, who was not one of the most starved-- we had all degrees, you know-- said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], I had more than enough to eat today.
He said I never thought I'd see the day when I'd say I had more than enough to eat, but I had more than enough to eat.
That felt good.
Well, I'm mentally-- maybe 1,000 in the typhus ward, and they kept coming in every day and dying.
I would guess somewhere-- it would fluctuate from 500 to 1,000.
Big building.
I just remember these long, long rows of cots.
I would guess from 500 to 1,000 most of the time.
You know, I was never sure of that.
There was some-- some machinery for taking care of them.
Their bodies were taken away.
And what was done about notifying kin, if they could find them, or burying them or what, you know, I really don't know.
We were so concerned with trying to save them-- the living that we just knew that, when people died, they were whisked off someplace.
I don't-- till you asked me that question, I hadn't thought about that.
But there was a bewildering variety of backgrounds when you talk to these people.
And they all spoke some German.
And since I speak fluent German, I did a lot of interpreting.
A Pole would talk to a Czech, would talked with this, would talk to me, and they'd go back down the chain so they could all communicate with each other.
Because like-- like most infections, except AIDS and rabies, a certain number of people will always recover.
They-- immune body, even under those conditions.
And especially the ones that hadn't been starved that long.
You see, you had all degrees of starvation.
Some people had only been there a month, and they were pretty thin, but they weren't terminally starved.
And they had a much better chance of living.
Not really.
We were pretty-- they didn't want us running around the camp, because we were in the typhus center.
Even though we had typhus shots, and we'd put DDT, there was an idea, stay where you are, don't go around and look at the rest of the building.
I've seen pictures of the gas chambers and the-- the crematoria.
I didn't actually see them myself.
We were not encouraged to wander around when we were in the typhus center.
Oh, I'm trying to think now.
So many stories I heard from these people, you know.
Well, I can remember a sense of hope that I heard among these people.
And they were-- it's hard to believe, but they were talking about what's the best political system for Europe and the world.
I mean, they were thinking even in the middle of this horror.
The Social Democrats are not a good idea.
We need to do this, we need to do that, and they'd get into political arguments.
Liana, don't let Alex in.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This big Russian kid's coming.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Go ahead.
Sorry.
Visitors at the door.
Yeah.
What-- what amazed me was that some of them said, well, we-- we figured we'd die, but we were sure some of us would live.
We sure you guys would get here some day.
And that was a very moving thing to hear.
And they had even-- when they had bits of paper or something to write on, they were writing notes on things as remote as philosophy and government, and on and on and on.
That's what I went away with.
There was an amazing sense of-- of courage that you saw in the survivors, when I finally left Dachau.
Two weeks.
By that time, the worst of it was over.
Those who were going to die had died, and they were getting food.
And I knew I had to get back to my unit, because we were going to be moving around, doing assorted things.

I got tapped, later on, the fighting was over, they broke up the combat units, and I was assigned to it-- because here I was major Medical Corps, fluent German, 7th Army headquarters in Heidelberg-- I got assigned to the-- an occupation job.
First I was made a commanding officer of a separate medical battalion, and we were going to go on to fight the Japanese, help the Japanese-- help fight the Japanese, in the China Burma India Theater.
We were retraining, as much as you could train for China, and since-- since I'm Germany.
And then, the big bomb got dropped, so we knew it was over.
So then, I got assigned to an occupation job.
I was in charge of civilian venereal disease in southwest Germany.

I had to set up 4,000 civilian venereal disease beds in southwest-- go into the bombed-out cities, find beds, find hospitals, round up doctors from the German prisoner war cages, and try and supply them.
It was kind of a chaotic job.
But I got to visit with the Germans and talk with them a lot.
What did you learn from that?
And what I learned from that is the-- I don't know if they're the biggest bunch of liars or deniers, or just myopic people in the world.
The attitudes of the Germans, rarely did they feel a lot of guilt about what had happened.
There was an attitude to blame everybody else, and especially in the Rhineland.
That was the [INAUDIBLE], north of Heidelberg.
You went up to [INAUDIBLE] toward the Rhine.
It was known as a hotbed of Nazi political activity in that part of Germany.
Down in Bavaria, there had been resistance.

When we-- we took the city of Munich-- we surrounded Munich, the Third Division took-- but scouts-- our scouts met people from a resistance outfit called the Bavarian Actions Unit, [SPEAKING GERMAN], who came out and said that they were trying to take over the city from the Nazis.
Bavaria was-- there was more of a resistance.
The Rhineland, rather, was hot bed Nazi country.
And the standard lines I heard were, we never suspected anything.
We didn't know what was going on.
How can we possibly dream of such a thing?
No question of denying it.
And the other thing we learned was, well, we're only little people.
We're just little people, what could we do about it?
How was that for you hearing that?
How did you react to that when they told you that?
Well, the reaction-- one reaction I had was in the bookstore at Dachau.
I wrote about this in my book.
The young Yugoslav wanted to get German-- English-- German-English Dictionary, because he was studying the language.
OK.
Yeah.
